Our Story

How it began

Friends in 2018. Years apart. Then one text brought us back together — and proved he was right all along about us being soulmates.

On the 17th of July, 2027, surrounded by the Aegean and everyone we love, we'll celebrate that story on the shores of Samos Island.

The Island

Samos, Greece

Samos is one of Greece's most beautiful islands — lush, mountainous, and steeped in history, just a stone's throw from the Turkish coast. We cannot think of a better place to bring everyone together.

By Air

Fly directly to Samos International Airport (SMI). Direct flights available from Athens, Brussels, Vienna, and several European cities in summer.

By Ferry

Regular connections from Piraeus (Athens), with stops at beautiful islands like Syros and Mykonos along the way. The journey takes approximately 8 hours overnight.

The Venue

Doryssa Seaside Resort

The wedding ceremony and reception will take place at the Doryssa Seaside Resort — a beautifully restored complex set directly on a private beach on the Aegean Sea in Pythagorion, Samos.

The Doryssa is a collection of distinct properties along the same stretch of coastline, each with its own character — from classic seaside resort to boutique townhouse to design retreat. Browse the gallery and find the one that suits you.

Doryssa Seaside Resort 1 / 7

Our Heritage

Two cultures, one celebration

Our wedding weaves together the richness of both Greek and Slovak tradition. Here is a little guide to what you might witness — and be invited to take part in — on the day.

Greek Traditions
Slovak Traditions

Money Dance Χορός με χρήματα

During the reception, guests pin banknotes onto the bride and groom as they dance together. A generous and lively tradition — and the best excuse for everyone to get onto the dance floor.

Koufeta Κουφέτα

Sugared almonds given to every guest as a wedding favour, always in an odd number — typically five — representing health, happiness, fertility, longevity, and wealth. The sugar coating is the sweetness of life; the bitter almond beneath, its challenges. Inseparable, like the couple.

Names on the Shoes Ονόματα στα παπούτσια

Before the ceremony, the names of unmarried friends and family are written on the sole of the bride's shoe. By the end of the night, whichever name has worn away the most is said to be the next to marry. A quiet competition that plays out entirely on the dance floor.

The Candle Lighting Η Λαμπάδα

Each guest entering the church receives a tall white candle — the lambáda — which is lit during the ceremony. As the couple exchanges their vows in candlelight, the warm glow of everyone they love surrounds them. It is one of the most quietly beautiful moments of the Orthodox wedding.

Rice Throwing Ρύζι

As the newlyweds emerge from the church, guests shower them with rice — a symbol of fertility, abundance, and a life full of good things. It is impossible not to laugh. It is impossible not to feel the love.

The First Dance Prvý Tanec

In Slovak tradition, the first dance belongs to the whole wedding. Guests circle the couple, singing and clapping, before the dancing opens to everyone. A moment of pure collective joy — and the signal that the real party has begun.

Redový Tanec The Line Dance

A beloved Slovak folk dance where guests form a long chain and wind through the room in a joyful procession. Led by the couple, it pulls everyone onto the floor — young and old alike — weaving the whole celebration into one moving, laughing line.

Breaking of the Plate Rozbíjanie taniera

A plate is broken on the ground before the couple, and together they sweep up the shards. It is said that sweeping side by side — tackling the mess as a team — is the first small act of their life together. A reminder that marriage is not only celebration, but also collaboration.

Spoločné Jedenie Polievky Sharing the Soup

Towards the end of the night, the couple shares a bowl of soup together — often a rich garlic broth or svadobná polievka. After hours of dancing, toasting, and celebrating, it is a quiet, intimate moment: just the two of them, a single bowl, and the understanding that from now on, every meal is shared.

Krajanie Torty Cutting the Cake

The cutting of the wedding cake is a beloved Slovak ritual — the couple cuts together, feeds each other the first slice, and the guests watch closely. How they handle the knife is said to reveal who will truly run the household. Choose wisely.

Hádzanie Svadobnej Kytice The Bouquet Toss

The bride turns her back to the gathered unmarried guests and tosses her bouquet over her shoulder. Whoever catches it is said to be the next to marry. It is one part tradition, one part chaos — and always one of the most spirited moments of the evening.

Dress Code

Wear something blue

Of course, wear whatever makes you feel wonderful — there are no rules and no obligations. But if you'd like to join us in a small gesture of unity, we would love to see a touch of blue. It is the colour of the Aegean, the colour of Slovak folk tradition, and the thread that runs through both of our stories. A navy suit, a cornflower dress, a blue pocket square — whatever feels like you.

We do kindly ask that guests avoid wearing black or white — white is reserved for the bride, and we'd love the celebration to feel as warm and colourful as the island itself.

Wedding Gift

Your presence is our gift

Travelling to Samos is already an incredibly generous gift to us, and having you there is everything we could wish for. We truly do not expect anything more.

However, if you feel moved to give something, a contribution towards our life together — our home, our travels, our future — would mean the world to us.
